chapters.title: Preparedness Foundations
chapters.summary: Core household readiness principles that stay usable offline and under stress.

sections.heading: Start With The Risks You Actually Face
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Preparedness works better when you plan against likely local problems first.

- List the disruptions your household is most likely to face.
- Prioritize water, light, communication, warmth, and essential medications before edge-case gear.
- Write down the first three actions for each likely disruption so they are still clear when everyone is tired or stressed.
sections.plainText: Preparedness works better when you plan against likely local problems first. List the disruptions your household is most likely to face. Prioritize water, light, communication, warmth, and essential medications before edge-case gear. Write down the first three actions for each likely disruption so they are still clear when everyone is tired or stressed.

sections.heading: Build Layers Instead Of Single-Point Fixes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A resilient plan has backups for the basics.

- Keep more than one light source and more than one charging option.
- Spread critical supplies across the home so one failure or one missed refill does not wipe out the whole plan.
- Review the plan on a fixed schedule instead of waiting for an emergency to reveal the gaps.
sections.plainText: A resilient plan has backups for the basics. Keep more than one light source and more than one charging option. Spread critical supplies across the home so one failure or one missed refill does not wipe out the whole plan. Review the plan on a fixed schedule instead of waiting for an emergency to reveal the gaps.

chapters.title: Family Plan And Emergency Contacts
chapters.summary: Household communication plans, meeting points, and emergency contact organization.

sections.heading: Build A Household Communication Plan
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A plan everyone knows is better than a perfect plan nobody remembers.

- Agree on one primary contact method. Texting is more reliable than calling during high-traffic events.
- Choose one out-of-area contact person everyone can check in with.
- Write the plan on a card each person carries, not just in a phone.
- Practice the plan at least once so everyone knows the steps when they are stressed.
sections.plainText: A plan everyone knows is better than a perfect plan nobody remembers. Agree on one primary contact method. Texting is more reliable than calling during high-traffic events. Choose one out-of-area contact person everyone can check in with. Write the plan on a card each person carries, not just in a phone. Practice the plan at least once so everyone knows the steps when they are stressed.

sections.heading: Choose Meeting Points And Routes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Pick two meeting points and make sure everyone can find them without a phone.

- **Primary:** A visible spot near your home, such as the mailbox or a neighbor's driveway.
- **Secondary:** A location outside your neighborhood in case the area is evacuated, such as a community center or school.
- Walk or drive both routes at least once.
- If children are in school or daycare, confirm the facility's reunification plan.
sections.plainText: Pick two meeting points and make sure everyone can find them without a phone. Primary: A visible spot near your home, such as the mailbox or a neighbor's driveway. Secondary: A location outside your neighborhood in case the area is evacuated, such as a community center or school. Walk or drive both routes at least once. If children are in school or daycare, confirm the facility's reunification plan.

sections.heading: Prepare Emergency Contact Lists
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Keep a written contact list that works when phones are dead or lost.

- Include household members, the out-of-area contact, neighbors, doctors, insurance, and your local utility companies.
- Print or write copies for each go-bag, the car, and one posted at home.
- Update the list every six months or after any change in phone numbers or addresses.
- Store a digital copy in a notes app that works offline.
sections.plainText: Keep a written contact list that works when phones are dead or lost. Include household members, the out-of-area contact, neighbors, doctors, insurance, and your local utility companies. Print or write copies for each go-bag, the car, and one posted at home. Update the list every six months or after any change in phone numbers or addresses. Store a digital copy in a notes app that works offline.

chapters.title: Water
chapters.summary: Safe water storage, rotation, and treatment for household preparedness.

sections.heading: Store Drinking Water Safely
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Water is the first supply to run short and the hardest to improvise safely.

- Store at least one gallon per person per day for a minimum of three days.
- Use food-grade containers with tight-fitting lids.
- Keep containers in a cool, dark place away from chemicals and direct sunlight.
- Label each container with the fill date.
- Do not reuse containers that held milk, juice, or other non-water liquids.
sections.plainText: Water is the first supply to run short and the hardest to improvise safely. Store at least one gallon per person per day for a minimum of three days. Use food-grade containers with tight-fitting lids. Keep containers in a cool, dark place away from chemicals and direct sunlight. Label each container with the fill date. Do not reuse containers that held milk, juice, or other non-water liquids.

sections.heading: Rotate And Test Stored Water
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Stored water stays safe for a long time if the containers are sealed and clean, but rotation keeps you confident in your supply.

- Replace stored water every six months, or sooner if containers show cloudiness, odor, or damage.
- Move the oldest containers to the front of storage so they get used first.
- Inspect seals and lids during each rotation.
- Write the next rotation date where you can see it without moving containers.
sections.plainText: Stored water stays safe for a long time if the containers are sealed and clean, but rotation keeps you confident in your supply. Replace stored water every six months, or sooner if containers show cloudiness, odor, or damage. Move the oldest containers to the front of storage so they get used first. Inspect seals and lids during each rotation. Write the next rotation date where you can see it without moving containers.

sections.heading: Treat Water When Supply Is Uncertain
sections.bodyMarkdown:
If your stored supply runs out or tap water safety is in question, treat water before drinking.

- **Boiling:** Bring water to a full rolling boil for at least one minute. Let it cool before storing.
- **Disinfection:** If you cannot boil, use unscented household chlorine bleach (6% sodium hypochlorite). Add 8 drops per gallon, stir, and wait 30 minutes. The water should have a slight chlorine smell.
- Do not use water from radiators, hot water boilers, swimming pools, or water beds for drinking.
- When in doubt about safety, use bottled water or wait for an official all-clear.
sections.plainText: If your stored supply runs out or tap water safety is in question, treat water before drinking. Boiling: Bring water to a full rolling boil for at least one minute. Let it cool before storing. Disinfection: If you cannot boil, use unscented household chlorine bleach (6% sodium hypochlorite). Add 8 drops per gallon, stir, and wait 30 minutes. The water should have a slight chlorine smell. Do not use water from radiators, hot water boilers, swimming pools, or water beds for drinking. When in doubt about safety, use bottled water or wait for an official all-clear.

chapters.title: Food
chapters.summary: Shelf-stable food planning, rotation, and safe handling during outages.

sections.heading: Build A Shelf-Stable Food Supply
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Stock food your household will actually eat, not just whatever has a long shelf life.

- Keep at least a three-day supply of non-perishable food per person.
- Choose items that require no refrigeration, little or no water to prepare, and no cooking if possible.
- Include canned proteins, nut butters, dried fruit, crackers, granola bars, and ready-to-eat cereals.
- Keep a manual can opener with the food supply.
- Account for dietary restrictions, allergies, infant formula, and pet food.
sections.plainText: Stock food your household will actually eat, not just whatever has a long shelf life. Keep at least a three-day supply of non-perishable food per person. Choose items that require no refrigeration, little or no water to prepare, and no cooking if possible. Include canned proteins, nut butters, dried fruit, crackers, granola bars, and ready-to-eat cereals. Keep a manual can opener with the food supply. Account for dietary restrictions, allergies, infant formula, and pet food.

sections.heading: Rotate And Track Stored Food
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Food you forget about is food you cannot trust.

- Check expiration dates every three months.
- Move older items to the front and use them in regular meals before they expire.
- Replace consumed or expired items promptly.
- Keep an inventory list near the storage area. The Inventory tab in this app can help track quantities and dates.
sections.plainText: Food you forget about is food you cannot trust. Check expiration dates every three months. Move older items to the front and use them in regular meals before they expire. Replace consumed or expired items promptly. Keep an inventory list near the storage area. The Inventory tab in this app can help track quantities and dates.

sections.heading: Manage Refrigerated Food During Outages
sections.bodyMarkdown:
The goal is to keep cold food safe as long as possible without opening the door unnecessarily.

- A closed refrigerator keeps food safe for about four hours.
- A full freezer holds temperature for roughly 48 hours; a half-full freezer for about 24 hours.
- Resist the urge to check. Each door opening shortens the safe window.
- If power has been out more than four hours, discard perishable items that have risen above 40°F (4°C).
- When in doubt, throw it out. Foodborne illness is a bigger risk than wasted groceries.
sections.plainText: The goal is to keep cold food safe as long as possible without opening the door unnecessarily. A closed refrigerator keeps food safe for about four hours. A full freezer holds temperature for roughly 48 hours; a half-full freezer for about 24 hours. Resist the urge to check. Each door opening shortens the safe window. If power has been out more than four hours, discard perishable items that have risen above 40 degrees F (4 degrees C). When in doubt, throw it out. Foodborne illness is a bigger risk than wasted groceries.

chapters.title: Power Outage
chapters.summary: Preparation, first-hour response, and device management during power outages.

sections.heading: Prepare Before An Outage
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Preparation before the lights go out is faster and cheaper than improvising afterward.

- Charge all phones, tablets, and battery banks when a storm or outage is expected.
- Set refrigerator and freezer to their coldest settings to extend the safe window.
- Fill bathtubs or large containers with water in case water pumps lose power.
- Place a flashlight or lantern in each occupied room.
- Unplug sensitive electronics to prevent surge damage when power returns.
sections.plainText: Preparation before the lights go out is faster and cheaper than improvising afterward. Charge all phones, tablets, and battery banks when a storm or outage is expected. Set refrigerator and freezer to their coldest settings to extend the safe window. Fill bathtubs or large containers with water in case water pumps lose power. Place a flashlight or lantern in each occupied room. Unplug sensitive electronics to prevent surge damage when power returns.

sections.heading: Manage The First Hours Without Power
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Stay calm, account for everyone, and stabilize the basics.

- Confirm everyone in the household is safe. Check on anyone who relies on powered medical equipment.
- Switch to battery-powered lighting. Avoid candles in routine indoor use.
- Keep the refrigerator and freezer doors closed.
- Check on neighbors, especially elderly or medically vulnerable people.
- Monitor a battery-powered or hand-crank NOAA weather radio for updates.
- Report the outage to your utility if it has not been acknowledged.
sections.plainText: Stay calm, account for everyone, and stabilize the basics. Confirm everyone in the household is safe. Check on anyone who relies on powered medical equipment. Switch to battery-powered lighting. Avoid candles in routine indoor use. Keep the refrigerator and freezer doors closed. Check on neighbors, especially elderly or medically vulnerable people. Monitor a battery-powered or hand-crank NOAA weather radio for updates. Report the outage to your utility if it has not been acknowledged.

sections.heading: Charge Devices And Conserve Battery
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Phones are your most important communication tool. Treat battery life as a critical resource.

- Switch phones to low-power mode immediately.
- Turn off Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and background app refresh if not needed.
- Reduce screen brightness to the minimum usable level.
- Charge from your largest battery bank first; save smaller portable chargers for later.
- A car charger works if the car is running, but never run a vehicle in a closed garage.
sections.plainText: Phones are your most important communication tool. Treat battery life as a critical resource. Switch phones to low-power mode immediately. Turn off Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and background app refresh if not needed. Reduce screen brightness to the minimum usable level. Charge from your largest battery bank first; save smaller portable chargers for later. A car charger works if the car is running, but never run a vehicle in a closed garage.

chapters.title: Cooking Without Power
chapters.summary: Safe outdoor cooking options and indoor limitations during power outages.

sections.heading: Safe Outdoor Cooking Options
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Outdoor cooking is the safest option when the power is out.

- Use a propane or charcoal grill outdoors only, at least 10 feet from structures.
- A portable camp stove with proper ventilation works well for simple meals.
- Never use charcoal or gas grills inside the house, garage, or any enclosed space.
- Keep fuel stored away from heat sources and out of direct sunlight.
- Have a fire extinguisher nearby when cooking with open flame.
sections.plainText: Outdoor cooking is the safest option when the power is out. Use a propane or charcoal grill outdoors only, at least 10 feet from structures. A portable camp stove with proper ventilation works well for simple meals. Never use charcoal or gas grills inside the house, garage, or any enclosed space. Keep fuel stored away from heat sources and out of direct sunlight. Have a fire extinguisher nearby when cooking with open flame.

sections.heading: Indoor Cooking Limitations And Risks
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Indoor cooking without proper ventilation creates serious carbon monoxide and fire risks.

- Do not use outdoor grills, camp stoves, or generators inside any enclosed space.
- Do not heat your home with a gas oven or stovetop.
- If you have a gas stove that still functions during a power outage, use it briefly for simple heating tasks with windows cracked for ventilation. Do not leave it unattended.
- Carbon monoxide is odorless and deadly. If your CO detector alarms, leave the building immediately and call 911.
- Prefer no-cook food options indoors when the power is out.
sections.plainText: Indoor cooking without proper ventilation creates serious carbon monoxide and fire risks. Do not use outdoor grills, camp stoves, or generators inside any enclosed space. Do not heat your home with a gas oven or stovetop. If you have a gas stove that still functions during a power outage, use it briefly for simple heating tasks with windows cracked for ventilation. Do not leave it unattended. Carbon monoxide is odorless and deadly. If your CO detector alarms, leave the building immediately and call 911. Prefer no-cook food options indoors when the power is out.

chapters.title: Warmth And Shelter
chapters.summary: Staying warm at home during cold-weather power outages.

sections.heading: Keep Your Home Warm Without Power
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Retaining heat is easier than generating it.

- Close off rooms you are not using and gather in one interior room.
- Hang blankets over windows and doorways to slow heat loss.
- If you have a fireplace or wood stove, use it according to the manufacturer's instructions. Keep the damper open while burning.
- Do not use gas ovens, grills, or portable generators for indoor heating.
- Let faucets drip slightly to prevent pipe freezing during extended cold outages.
sections.plainText: Retaining heat is easier than generating it. Close off rooms you are not using and gather in one interior room. Hang blankets over windows and doorways to slow heat loss. If you have a fireplace or wood stove, use it according to the manufacturer's instructions. Keep the damper open while burning. Do not use gas ovens, grills, or portable generators for indoor heating. Let faucets drip slightly to prevent pipe freezing during extended cold outages.

sections.heading: Dress For Cold Indoors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Layering is more effective than one heavy garment.

- Wear a base layer of moisture-wicking fabric against your skin.
- Add an insulating middle layer such as fleece or wool.
- Top with a windproof or water-resistant outer layer if moving outside.
- Wear a warm hat indoors. A large percentage of body heat escapes through the head.
- Keep feet warm with wool socks and insulated slippers.
- Sleeping bags rated for cold weather are more effective than stacked blankets.
sections.plainText: Layering is more effective than one heavy garment. Wear a base layer of moisture-wicking fabric against your skin. Add an insulating middle layer such as fleece or wool. Top with a windproof or water-resistant outer layer if moving outside. Wear a warm hat indoors. A large percentage of body heat escapes through the head. Keep feet warm with wool socks and insulated slippers. Sleeping bags rated for cold weather are more effective than stacked blankets.

sections.heading: Choose And Prepare A Warm Room
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Concentrating people and heat in one room makes a big difference.

- Pick a small interior room with the fewest exterior walls and windows.
- Close doors and vents to unused rooms.
- Gather blankets, sleeping bags, pillows, flashlights, water, and snacks in the room.
- If possible, choose a room with a south-facing window to gain daytime solar warmth.
- Keep pets in the room with you for shared warmth and their safety.
sections.plainText: Concentrating people and heat in one room makes a big difference. Pick a small interior room with the fewest exterior walls and windows. Close doors and vents to unused rooms. Gather blankets, sleeping bags, pillows, flashlights, water, and snacks in the room. If possible, choose a room with a south-facing window to gain daytime solar warmth. Keep pets in the room with you for shared warmth and their safety.

chapters.title: First Aid, Hygiene, And Medications
chapters.summary: Kit organization, medication management, and hygiene without running water.

sections.heading: Organize A First Aid Kit
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A well-organized kit is faster to use when hands are shaking.

- Stock adhesive bandages, gauze pads, medical tape, antiseptic wipes, and disposable gloves.
- Include tweezers, scissors, a digital thermometer, and an instant cold pack.
- Add pain relievers, antihistamines, and anti-diarrheal medication.
- Check expiration dates every three months and replace anything used or expired.
- Keep one kit at home and a smaller version in each vehicle and go-bag.
sections.plainText: A well-organized kit is faster to use when hands are shaking. Stock adhesive bandages, gauze pads, medical tape, antiseptic wipes, and disposable gloves. Include tweezers, scissors, a digital thermometer, and an instant cold pack. Add pain relievers, antihistamines, and anti-diarrheal medication. Check expiration dates every three months and replace anything used or expired. Keep one kit at home and a smaller version in each vehicle and go-bag.

sections.heading: Manage Medications During Disruptions
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Running out of a critical medication during a disruption is a serious risk. Plan ahead.

- Keep at least a seven-day supply of each prescription medication on hand at all times.
- Store medications in a cool, dry place. Avoid leaving them in vehicles where heat and cold extremes can degrade them.
- Maintain a written list of all medications, dosages, and prescribing doctors for each household member.
- Ask your pharmacist about emergency refill policies in your area.
- If you depend on refrigerated medication such as insulin, plan a cold-storage backup.
- **Caution:** This app does not provide dosage advice or medical diagnosis. Consult your healthcare provider for medication-specific guidance.
sections.plainText: Running out of a critical medication during a disruption is a serious risk. Plan ahead. Keep at least a seven-day supply of each prescription medication on hand at all times. Store medications in a cool, dry place. Avoid leaving them in vehicles where heat and cold extremes can degrade them. Maintain a written list of all medications, dosages, and prescribing doctors for each household member. Ask your pharmacist about emergency refill policies in your area. If you depend on refrigerated medication such as insulin, plan a cold-storage backup. Caution: This app does not provide dosage advice or medical diagnosis. Consult your healthcare provider for medication-specific guidance.

sections.heading: Maintain Hygiene Without Running Water
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Good hygiene prevents illness when medical help may be delayed.

- Stock hand sanitizer with at least 60% alcohol for when soap and water are not available.
- Keep baby wipes or wet wipes for basic body cleaning.
- Use a bucket and small amount of water for a sponge bath if water is limited.
- Dispose of waste carefully. If toilets do not flush, use a lined bucket with a tight lid and kitty litter or sawdust to manage odor.
- Wash hands before handling food and after using the toilet, even with limited water.
sections.plainText: Good hygiene prevents illness when medical help may be delayed. Stock hand sanitizer with at least 60% alcohol for when soap and water are not available. Keep baby wipes or wet wipes for basic body cleaning. Use a bucket and small amount of water for a sponge bath if water is limited. Dispose of waste carefully. If toilets do not flush, use a lined bucket with a tight lid and kitty litter or sawdust to manage odor. Wash hands before handling food and after using the toilet, even with limited water.

chapters.title: Go-Bags
chapters.summary: Building, customizing, and maintaining evacuation go-bags for your household.

sections.heading: Build A 72-Hour Go-Bag
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A go-bag gives you 72 hours of self-sufficiency when you need to leave quickly.

- Use a durable backpack that is comfortable to carry for extended walks.
- Pack water (at least one liter), high-calorie food bars, and water purification tablets.
- Include a flashlight, batteries, a phone charger, and a whistle.
- Add copies of important documents, cash in small bills, and a list of emergency contacts.
- Include a change of weather-appropriate clothing and sturdy shoes.
- Keep the bag near your door or in a consistent, easy-to-grab location.
sections.plainText: A go-bag gives you 72 hours of self-sufficiency when you need to leave quickly. Use a durable backpack that is comfortable to carry for extended walks. Pack water (at least one liter), high-calorie food bars, and water purification tablets. Include a flashlight, batteries, a phone charger, and a whistle. Add copies of important documents, cash in small bills, and a list of emergency contacts. Include a change of weather-appropriate clothing and sturdy shoes. Keep the bag near your door or in a consistent, easy-to-grab location.

sections.heading: Customize Bags For Family Members
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Each person's bag should reflect their specific needs.

- **Children:** Include comfort items, extra snacks, a small toy, and any school-provided emergency info.
- **Infants:** Pack formula, diapers, wipes, and a change of clothes.
- **Elderly or mobility-impaired:** Include prescription medications, mobility aids, and a list of medical conditions.
- **Pets:** Pack food, water, a leash or carrier, vaccination records, and any medications.
- Every bag should have the household meeting point written on a card inside.
sections.plainText: Each person's bag should reflect their specific needs. Children: Include comfort items, extra snacks, a small toy, and any school-provided emergency info. Infants: Pack formula, diapers, wipes, and a change of clothes. Elderly or mobility-impaired: Include prescription medications, mobility aids, and a list of medical conditions. Pets: Pack food, water, a leash or carrier, vaccination records, and any medications. Every bag should have the household meeting point written on a card inside.

sections.heading: Maintain And Rotate Go-Bag Contents
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A go-bag only works if the contents are current.

- Check the bag every three months.
- Replace expired food, water, and medications.
- Swap seasonal clothing to match the current weather.
- Verify that documents, contact lists, and cash are still accurate.
- Test the flashlight and recharge or replace battery banks.
- Adjust for any changes in household members, medications, or needs.
sections.plainText: A go-bag only works if the contents are current. Check the bag every three months. Replace expired food, water, and medications. Swap seasonal clothing to match the current weather. Verify that documents, contact lists, and cash are still accurate. Test the flashlight and recharge or replace battery banks. Adjust for any changes in household members, medications, or needs.

chapters.title: Home Supplies, Utilities, And Tools
chapters.summary: Essential household tools, utility shutoff awareness, and readiness item maintenance.

sections.heading: Essential Home Tools And Supplies
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A small set of reliable tools covers most disruption scenarios.

- Keep a multi-tool or basic toolkit with pliers, screwdrivers, an adjustable wrench, and a utility knife.
- Stock duct tape, plastic sheeting, zip ties, and work gloves.
- Have a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for emergency broadcasts.
- Keep a fire extinguisher rated for kitchen and electrical fires, and know where it is.
- Store tarps for temporary weather protection of broken windows or roof damage.
sections.plainText: A small set of reliable tools covers most disruption scenarios. Keep a multi-tool or basic toolkit with pliers, screwdrivers, an adjustable wrench, and a utility knife. Stock duct tape, plastic sheeting, zip ties, and work gloves. Have a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for emergency broadcasts. Keep a fire extinguisher rated for kitchen and electrical fires, and know where it is. Store tarps for temporary weather protection of broken windows or roof damage.

sections.heading: Know Your Utility Shutoffs
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Knowing where to shut off utilities can prevent a small problem from becoming a dangerous one.

- **Water main:** Usually near the street or where the supply line enters the house. Locate the valve and test it.
- **Gas shutoff:** Typically at the meter. Requires a wrench. Keep one nearby.
- **Electrical main breaker:** Inside the main panel, usually in a garage, basement, or utility room.
- Label each shutoff clearly so any household member can find it.
- **Caution:** Only shut off gas if you smell a leak or are told to by authorities. Do not turn it back on yourself. Call the utility company.
- Do not attempt electrical repairs beyond switching breakers unless you are trained.
sections.plainText: Knowing where to shut off utilities can prevent a small problem from becoming a dangerous one. Water main: Usually near the street or where the supply line enters the house. Locate the valve and test it. Gas shutoff: Typically at the meter. Requires a wrench. Keep one nearby. Electrical main breaker: Inside the main panel, usually in a garage, basement, or utility room. Label each shutoff clearly so any household member can find it. Caution: Only shut off gas if you smell a leak or are told to by authorities. Do not turn it back on yourself. Call the utility company. Do not attempt electrical repairs beyond switching breakers unless you are trained.

sections.heading: Maintain Household Readiness Items
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Supplies that are not maintained are supplies that fail when you need them.

- Test smoke detectors and carbon monoxide detectors monthly. Replace batteries yearly.
- Check the fire extinguisher pressure gauge quarterly.
- Inspect tarps, plastic sheeting, and tape for damage.
- Verify that work gloves and protective gear are still in usable condition.
- Keep an up-to-date list of home readiness items in the Inventory tab of this app.
sections.plainText: Supplies that are not maintained are supplies that fail when you need them. Test smoke detectors and carbon monoxide detectors monthly. Replace batteries yearly. Check the fire extinguisher pressure gauge quarterly. Inspect tarps, plastic sheeting, and tape for damage. Verify that work gloves and protective gear are still in usable condition. Keep an up-to-date list of home readiness items in the Inventory tab of this app.

chapters.title: Fire And Lighting
chapters.summary: Safe lighting options, battery management, and home fire safety.

sections.heading: Safe Lighting Options For Power Outages
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Good lighting reduces accidents and stress during an outage.

- LED flashlights and headlamps are the safest and most efficient option.
- Battery-powered lanterns provide ambient light for a room without fire risk.
- Glow sticks are a safe, no-heat option for children and bathrooms.
- If you use candles, place them on stable, heat-resistant surfaces away from anything flammable. Never leave candles unattended.
- Avoid oil lamps indoors unless they are designed for indoor use and you are familiar with safe operation.
sections.plainText: Good lighting reduces accidents and stress during an outage. LED flashlights and headlamps are the safest and most efficient option. Battery-powered lanterns provide ambient light for a room without fire risk. Glow sticks are a safe, no-heat option for children and bathrooms. If you use candles, place them on stable, heat-resistant surfaces away from anything flammable. Never leave candles unattended. Avoid oil lamps indoors unless they are designed for indoor use and you are familiar with safe operation.

sections.heading: Battery And Fuel Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Batteries and fuel are finite. Manage them like the critical resources they are.

- Standardize on common battery sizes (AA, AAA) to simplify stocking.
- Store batteries in a cool, dry place. Remove batteries from devices not in regular use to prevent corrosion.
- Rechargeable batteries and a solar charger extend your supply during multi-day outages.
- For fuel-based lighting or cooking, store fuel in approved containers away from living areas.
- Track battery and fuel inventory so you know what you have before an outage starts.
sections.plainText: Batteries and fuel are finite. Manage them like the critical resources they are. Standardize on common battery sizes (AA, AAA) to simplify stocking. Store batteries in a cool, dry place. Remove batteries from devices not in regular use to prevent corrosion. Rechargeable batteries and a solar charger extend your supply during multi-day outages. For fuel-based lighting or cooking, store fuel in approved containers away from living areas. Track battery and fuel inventory so you know what you have before an outage starts.

sections.heading: Fire Safety In The Home
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Fire risk increases when normal routines change during a disruption.

- Install smoke detectors on every level of the home, inside bedrooms, and outside sleeping areas. Test monthly.
- Install carbon monoxide detectors near sleeping areas and on every level.
- Keep a fire extinguisher in the kitchen and know how to use it. Pull the pin, aim at the base of the fire, squeeze the handle, sweep side to side.
- Never leave open flames, cooking, or space heaters unattended.
- Replace detector batteries at least once a year. Replace the entire unit every 10 years.
- **Caution:** If a fire is beyond what a single extinguisher can control, leave the building immediately and call 911. Do not attempt to fight a structural fire.
sections.plainText: Fire risk increases when normal routines change during a disruption. Install smoke detectors on every level of the home, inside bedrooms, and outside sleeping areas. Test monthly. Install carbon monoxide detectors near sleeping areas and on every level. Keep a fire extinguisher in the kitchen and know how to use it. Pull the pin, aim at the base of the fire, squeeze the handle, sweep side to side. Never leave open flames, cooking, or space heaters unattended. Replace detector batteries at least once a year. Replace the entire unit every 10 years. Caution: If a fire is beyond what a single extinguisher can control, leave the building immediately and call 911. Do not attempt to fight a structural fire.
